findings.h: Speculative decoding is not a free lunch at batch 1
findings.p: NVIDIA's 15× comes from a 120B model at high concurrency. With one user and a fast 7B, generation is already memory-bandwidth-bound — the 5060 Ti runs ~84.7 tok/s on its own. A draft model only helps if it guesses well enough to beat that, so the win is real but measured in tens of percent, not multiples — and only on the right tasks.

findings.h: The biggest surprise: speculation's overhead can backfire on a fast model
findings.p: Speculative decoding isn't free — each draft-then-verify cycle carries overhead, and on an already-fast target it can cost more than it saves. On Blackwell's quick 7B (~84.7 tok/s) the stock CUDA build's draft path collapsed to 0.27× of baseline — a 3–4× slowdown at perfectly healthy acceptance rates. The same CUDA build, same card, pointed at a slower 14B hit 1.57× on code — the heavier model gives the overhead something to amortize against. The eight-year-old Pascal 1080 Ti never collapsed on the 7B either, since it only runs ~50.5 tok/s. The Vulkan build carries far less speculative overhead (0.95× overall on the 7B, where CUDA managed only 0.27×). Rule of thumb: the faster your model already runs, the more the runtime choice matters — on a fast small model, prefer Vulkan or model-free n-gram.

findings.h: Speedups are entirely task-dependent — NVIDIA's exact shape
findings.p: Predictable text wins. The best working configs climbed to 1.23× on code/math but fell below 1.0× on open-ended chat, because the tiny draft model nails structured output and flails on free-form prose. Acceptance rate is the whole game — match the method to the workload.

findings.h: N-gram speculation is the free safety net
findings.p: It needs no second model and almost no memory, predicts repeats straight from your context, and landed right around 1.0× across the suite on both cards and both runtimes — it rarely hurts and sometimes helps on repetitive or structured output. Little reason not to leave it on.

findings.h: For memory-bound decoding, the generation gap is smaller than the spec sheet
findings.p: The 2017 GTX 1080 Ti baselined at ~57.3 tok/s (Vulkan) versus the 2025 5060 Ti's ~84.7 — only about 1.48×, despite eight years and a generational leap, because single-user decoding is bound by memory bandwidth (where the two cards are far closer than their age suggests), not raw compute. Worth knowing before you upgrade just to run a local model faster.

findings.h: The bigger the model, the bigger the win — and a 14B does fit
findings.p: Speculation pays off most on slow models, which is the whole reason NVIDIA runs it on a 120B. We see the same curve in miniature: moving from the 7B to a full-quality 14B (Q4_K_M) on the 5060 Ti, draft speculation on code jumped from 0.27× to 1.57×. And contrary to our first assumption, the 14B + draft fits in 16 GB with room to spare (~10 GB used at 4K context) — the weights are only ~9 GB; it's the KV cache and context length, not the model, that you budget for. Keep the context sane (or quantize the KV cache with -ctk q8_0) and a 14B speculates happily on a consumer card.
